title: Ferrari's championship lead becomes the paddock benchmark
meta: Published: 26 March 2026 | By Bernard “Bernie” Fletcher, Veteran Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: Ferrari sets the standard.
body:
It’s 2026 and we’re two races into a brand‑new season.

Already, the paddock has found its benchmark. Ferrari. Again.

I’ve seen this film before. In 2002, Schumacher won 11 of 17 races and half the grid talked about catching up by Spain. They never did. In 2004, the scarlet cars won 15 of 18, and by round three the championship was effectively a private contest between the two red cockpits. This isn’t that — not yet — but the shape of it is familiar. After Melbourne and Shanghai, Ferrari are no longer just a strong contender; they are the operation everyone else is measuring themselves against, and finding themselves short.

Total mastery in the wet. Unparalleled strategic brilliance in the dry. Two completely different demands, one winner. The rest of the grid is playing catch‑up, and most of them don’t even know which compound to chase.

What should worry the competition is not the wins themselves. It’s the versatility. In Australia, the Ferrari was planted through standing water and transition phases that caught out more celebrated names. In China, it showed tyre degradation characteristics that extended the hard‑compound window beyond what Red Bull, Mercedes, or McLaren could manage. A car that works everywhere is a car that will win everywhere. That’s not analysis; that’s physics.

But the constructor’s fight isn’t wrapped up. It rarely is in March. The glaring weakness in the Scuderia’s armour sits on the other side of the garage. Afonso Cachinho has struggled immensely to adapt, with no points, two Q1 exits, and a penalty collection that would make a rookie blush. Ferrari lead the constructors’ table on the strength of one driver’s flawless heroics. That works until it doesn’t. If Kevu, Roy, or a resurgent Mercedes lineup can consistently put two cars in the top five, Ferrari’s grip on the team championship will start to slip. A one‑legged stool never stood for long.

Japan will tell us whether this lead is structural or circumstantial. Suzuka has a habit of exposing aerodynamic weaknesses that other circuits forgive. The first sector punishes cars that lack front‑end bite. The high‑speed changes of direction through the Esses demand a chassis that responds instantly. If Ferrari sail through that test too, the rest of the paddock can start planning for 2027. If they falter, the season gets interesting again.

Right now, every other team is hoping for the latter. I’ve been around long enough to know that hope is not a strategy.

On to the next one, then.
